1: Psalm 140 bears the superscription "For the director of music, a Psalm of David" — placing it among the final Davidic psalms in the Psalter (Pss 138–145 form a cluster, after the long Asaphite, Korahite, and Hallel collections). The request "rescue me from evil men; protect me from violent men (ʾîš ḥămāsîm)" invokes the classic Psalter opposition between the righteous speaker and the violent aggressors. Ḥāmās (violence, injustice, wrong) is a legal term for the wrongful violence that destroys social order — it appears in the flood narrative (Gen 6:11, 13) as the definitive reason for God's judgment on the pre-flood world.
2: "Who scheme wickedness in their hearts and stir up conflict every day" — yāgûrû mĕlḥāmôt (they stir up/gather wars). The movement from heart-scheming to daily conflict-rousing is the two-stage description of the wicked in the Psalter's moral anthropology: wickedness begins as an interior orientation of the heart (cf. Ps 36:1–4, where the wicked "set no good thing before their eyes... they lie awake plotting wickedness") before expressing as social violence. The ANE wisdom tradition (Mesopotamian and Egyptian) also located the source of destructive behavior in the disordered heart.
3: "Their tongues are as sharp as a serpent's; viper venom is under their lips" — ḥiddĕpû lĕšônām kĕmô nāḥāš (they have sharpened their tongue like a serpent). The serpent-tongue imagery draws on the Genesis 3 narrative (the serpent's speech as the instrument of humanity's undoing) and the ANE wisdom tradition in which the tongue of the wicked is a deadly weapon. The same image appears in Ps 58:4 (the deaf cobra) and is cited by Paul in Romans 3:13 as part of his catena of OT texts demonstrating universal human guilt. Selah marks a pause to reflect on the severity of the threat.
4: "Keep me, O LORD, from the clutches of the wicked; preserve me from violent men who intend to make me stumble" — mĕzammĕmû lidhôt peʿāmāy (they plot to push aside my steps). The language of "pushing aside my steps" is a hunting metaphor: the prey is driven off the path and into the trap. The request for divine keeping (šāmĕrênî) and preservation (nĕṣurênî) uses the standard vocabulary of covenant protection — the same terms applied to the Torah's preservation of the righteous (Ps 119:11, 17) and to divine keeping of the traveler (Ps 121:7).
5: "The arrogant have laid a trap for me and stretched out cords; they have spread a net along my path and set snares" — the hunting imagery is elaborated: trap (paḥ), cords (ḥăbālîm), net (rešet), and snares (môqĕšîm) are the four implements of the fowler or hunter applied to the wicked surrounding the righteous. The same arsenal appears in Pss 9:15; 35:7–8; 57:6; 91:3; 141:9–10. The setting of elaborate hunting traps — hidden, covered, lying in wait on the exact path the prey walks — is the image for the calculated malice of those who pursue the righteous. Selah.
6: "I say to the LORD, 'You are my God' — hear me, O LORD, and listen to my pleas for mercy" — the confession "you are my God (ʾēlî ʾattāh)" is the covenant declaration of loyalty, the inverse of the enemies' malice. The same declaration appears in Ps 31:14 and is echoed in Ps 22:10 ("You have been my God from my mother's womb"). In the context of Psalms 140–144's sequence of prayer against enemies, this personal covenant confession grounds each prayer not in merit but in relationship.
7: "O Sovereign LORD, the strength of my salvation — you have shielded my head in the day of battle" — ʾadonāy YHWH (Sovereign LORD) — the double divine title emphasizing both lordship and covenant name. "The strength of my salvation (ʿōz yĕšûʿātî)" — salvation not as mere deliverance but as the divine power that accomplishes it. "Shielded my head in the day of battle (sakōtāh lĕrōʾšî bĕyôm nešeq)" — the military context: the shield that protects the head from sword blows. In the ANE warrior culture, the head was the most vulnerable and most honored part of the body; to protect the head was to preserve life and honor simultaneously.
8: "Do not give the wicked what they want, O LORD; do not let their evil plans succeed, or they will grow proud" — al-tēn YHWH maʾăwiyyê rāšāʿ (do not grant the desires of the wicked). The prayer restrains the enemy not only from their acts but from their desires being satisfied — since a satisfied enemy grows proud (yārûmû) and more dangerous. The logic of the imprecatory psalms is partly social-psychological: unanswered wickedness emboldens further wickedness, while divine intervention disrupts the cycle. Selah.
9: "Let the trouble caused by the words of those who hem me in fall back upon their own heads" — the principle of retributive return: the evil the enemies speak against the righteous should recoil upon themselves. This is the lex talionis applied to speech: words of harm returning to harm the speaker. The same principle appears in Ps 7:15–16 (the wicked "digs a pit and falls into it; his trouble returns to his own head") and Prov 26:27 ("Whoever digs a pit will fall into it"). The language of "those who hem me in (mĕkabbāy)" suggests besieging armies who surround the city or the prey with no escape.
10: "Let burning coals rain down on them; let them be thrown into the fire — into pits so deep they never rise" — the threefold imprecatory wish: burning coals (gḥālîm), fire (ʾēš), and deep pits (maḥămōrôt). Burning coals from heaven appear in theophanic contexts (Ps 18:13: "The LORD thundered from heaven... He shot arrows and scattered them; he hurled lightning bolts and routed them") — divine judgment expressed through natural forces deployed supernaturally. The "deep pits" echo the imagery of Sheol as the place from which none return (Ps 88:6; Isa 14:15). The imprecation asks for nothing more than the standard covenant curses — what YHWH swore he would do to covenant-breakers.
11: "Let no slander take root in the land; may evil relentlessly pursue the violent man until he falls" — ʾîš lāšôn bal-yikkôn bāʾāreṣ (a man of tongue will not be established in the land). Lāšôn (tongue/speech) stands metonymically for the slanderer and the violent speaker. The wisdom tradition holds that the slanderer destroys community (Prov 11:9; 16:28; 26:20–22). "Take root (yikkôn)" — the same verb used for the divine establishment of Zion and the Davidic throne; the slanderer's attempt at permanence is denied by the covenant structure of reality. The contrast: the righteous are established; the violent man is hunted down (yĕṣûdennû).
12: "I know that the LORD will uphold the cause of the oppressed and defend the rights of the poor" — the transition from imprecation to confident confession. "I know (yādaʿtî)" — the knowledge is not theological speculation but covenant testimony. "The cause of the oppressed (dîn ʿānî)" — dîn is a legal term for the formal adjudication of a case in court; YHWH is the divine judge who takes up the legal cause of those without human advocates (the ʿānî, the poor-and-lowly; and ʾebyônîm, the destitute). The Psalter consistently grounds the expectation of divine justice in covenant character (Ps 9:9; 10:14; 35:10; 72:4, 12–13; 82:3–4).
13: "Surely the righteous will give thanks to your name; the upright will live in your presence" — the psalm ends with a confident eschatological assertion rather than a resolved situation. "The upright will live in your presence (yēšĕbû yĕšārîm et-pānêkā)" — to dwell before the divine face is the ultimate covenant blessing (Num 6:25–26; Pss 11:7; 16:11; 17:15). The verb yāšab (to sit/dwell) echoes the throne-room language: the righteous have a permanent place in the divine presence, while the wicked are cast out. The psalm moves from peril (vv. 1–5) through prayer (vv. 6–11) to confidence (vv. 12–13).
